1:
"I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service." The witnesses note the great appeal to consecration, grounded in God's mercies. Chrysostom notes that, after discoursing at large on the love of God toward man and his unspeakable concern, Paul puts forward this exhortation to persuade those who received the benefit to live worthy of the gift; and though so great a person, he does not decline to beseech them — not for any gain to himself, but for theirs. The witnesses note Paul beseeching believers, by the mercies of God, to present their bodies a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable — their reasonable service; the whole consecration of the redeemed life, grounded in the mercies displayed in the gospel (Romans 1-11), the body offered as a living, holy sacrifice in reasonable worship. The witnesses note present your bodies. The witnesses note the living sacrifice.

The witnesses note the appeal to present the body a living sacrifice, by God's mercies — the whole consecration of the redeemed life.

So the witnesses make the verse the great appeal to consecration. The lesson the expositors draw is the whole consecration of the redeemed life, grounded in the mercies of God. The witnesses note the ground: "by the mercies of God" — Chrysostom notes Paul, having discoursed of God's love and mercy (Romans 1-11), now beseeches by those mercies; the appeal grounded in mercy; the gospel the motive; the mercies the constraint; the love the spur. The expositors observe the beseeching: "I beseech you therefore, brethren" — Paul beseeches (entreats, not merely commands); Chrysostom notes he does not decline to beseech, not for his own gain but theirs; the entreaty; the tender appeal; the apostle's beseeching. The witnesses press the sacrifice: "present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God" — the believer is to present the body a living sacrifice (not slain like the Old Testament victims, but living, devoted), holy (set apart, pure) and acceptable to God; the consecration; the body offered; the living, holy sacrifice; the whole self devoted. The expositors draw the lesson on the consecration of the body: the body — the members, the faculties, the whole bodily life — is to be presented to God, devoted to his service; the consecration; the body for the Lord (1 Corinthians 6:20); the whole life offered; the living sacrifice. The witnesses observe "reasonable service": "which is your reasonable service" — the witnesses note the sense — reasonable (rational, befitting a reasonable creature), or spiritual (of the mind and heart, not mere outward ritual); the reasonable/spiritual worship; the service of the renewed reason; the worship in spirit and truth; the rational devotion. The expositors note the motive of mercy: the consecration is grounded in mercy — the only fitting response to the mercies of God; the mercy the motive; the love constraining (2 Corinthians 5:14); the gospel the spring of the holy life; the mercies moving to devotion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the present your bodies, the living sacrifice, and the appeal by God's mercies. The verse thus records the appeal to present the body a living sacrifice — teaching the whole consecration of the redeemed life, grounded in the mercies of God.
2:
"And be not conformed to this world: but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind, that ye may prove what is that good, and acceptable, and perfect, will of God." The witnesses note the call to transformation, not conformity. Gill notes "this world" may mean the Mosaic dispensation (the worldly sanctuary now passed), or the present evil age with its customs, maxims, and fashions, to which believers are not to conform. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers not to be conformed to this world but transformed by the renewing of the mind, that they may prove the will of God; the two opposed — the outward conformity to the world's fashion and the inward transformation by the renewed mind, the renewal that issues in the discernment and proving of God's good, acceptable, and perfect will; the transformed life. The witnesses note not conformed to this world. The witnesses note transformed by the renewed mind.

The witnesses note the call not to be conformed to the world but transformed by the renewed mind — proving the will of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to transformation. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between outward conformity to the world and inward transformation by the renewed mind, issuing in the proving of God's will. The witnesses note the negative: "be not conformed to this world" — Gill notes "this world" may mean the Mosaic dispensation or the present evil age with its customs and fashions; believers are not to conform to the world's fashion, maxims, and ways; the non-conformity; the not-fashioning to the world; the world's mould refused. The expositors observe the positive: "be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind" — the believer is to be transformed (changed in nature, not merely fashioned outwardly) by the renewing of the mind; the transformation; the inward change; the renewed mind; the metamorphosis of the soul. The witnesses press the contrast of conformity and transformation: conformity is outward (fashioning to the world's pattern); transformation is inward (the changed nature, the renewed mind); the two opposed — the world's mould and the Spirit's renewal; the outward and the inward; the fashion and the change. The expositors draw the lesson on the renewing of the mind: the transformation is "by the renewing of the mind" — the Spirit renewing the understanding, the affections, the will; the mind renewed; the inward renewal; the new pattern of thought; the renewed reason; the transformed mind the spring of the transformed life. The witnesses observe the proving of God's will: "that ye may prove what is that good, and acceptable, and perfect, will of God" — the renewed mind proves (discerns, tests, approves) the will of God — good, acceptable, perfect; the proving; the discernment; the renewed mind knowing God's will; the spiritual discernment; the will approved. The expositors note the threefold description of God's will: God's will is good (beneficial), acceptable (well-pleasing), and perfect (complete); the threefold; the excellence of God's will; the will to be proved; the good and perfect will. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not conformed to this world, the transformed by the renewed mind, and the proving of God's will. The verse thus records the call to transformation — teaching the contrast of conformity and transformation, the renewed mind issuing in the proving of God's will.
3:
"For I say, through the grace given unto me, to every man that is among you, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think; but to think soberly, according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith." The witnesses note the call to sober self-estimation. Gill notes Paul speaks "through the grace given" — the grace of apostleship, the authority received from Christ to give instructions to the churches. The witnesses note Paul charging every man not to think of himself more highly than he ought, but to think soberly, according to the measure of faith God has dealt; the warning against pride and self-conceit, the call to humble, sober self-estimation, measuring oneself not by self-flattery but by the gifts and faith God has apportioned; the humility that becomes the member of the body. The witnesses note not think too highly. The witnesses note think soberly.

The witnesses note the call to sober self-estimation — not thinking too highly, but according to the measure of faith God has dealt.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to sober self-estimation. The lesson the expositors draw is the warning against pride and the call to humble, sober self-estimation according to the gifts God has apportioned. The witnesses note the authority: "through the grace given unto me" — Gill notes Paul speaks through the grace of apostleship, the authority received from Christ; the apostolic authority; the grace given; the warrant of the charge; the apostle's commission. The expositors observe the warning: "not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think" — the warning against thinking too highly of oneself; the pride; the self-conceit; the overestimation; the inflated self-regard; the haughtiness reproved. The witnesses press the call to soberness: "but to think soberly" — the believer is to think soberly (with sound judgment, modesty, humility) of himself; the sober self-estimation; the modest judgment; the humble assessment; the sound mind about self. The expositors draw the lesson on the measure of faith: "according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith" — each is to estimate himself according to the measure of faith (and gifts) God has dealt; the measure apportioned; the gifts from God; the not-self-made; the measuring by God's gift, not self-flattery; the apportioned faith. The witnesses observe the humility before God's gift: whatever a man has (faith, gifts, abilities) is dealt by God — so there is no ground for pride, but for humble stewardship; the humility; the gift not earned; "what hast thou that thou didst not receive?" (1 Corinthians 4:7); the gift humbling. The expositors note the connection to the body (v.4-5): the sober self-estimation prepares for the teaching on the body — each member with its gift, none to be proud, none to despise; the humility that becomes the member; the body needing the humble estimate; the gifts for the whole. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not think too highly, the think soberly, and the measure of faith. The verse thus records the call to sober self-estimation — teaching the warning against pride and the humble estimate according to the gifts God has dealt.
4:
"For as we have many members in one body, and all members have not the same office:" The witnesses note the figure of the one body with many members. Chrysostom notes Paul uses the same illustration as to the Corinthians, to allay the same passion of haughtiness — for great is its power to correct the disease of pride: are we not all one body, both great and small, and members one of another? why then by haughtiness separate thyself? The witnesses note Paul illustrating from the body — many members in one body, and not all with the same office; the figure that grounds both humility and unity, the diversity of the members and their offices showing that each has his own place and function, none superior, none useless; the body the pattern of the church. The witnesses note many members. The witnesses note not the same office.

The witnesses note the figure of the one body with many members and diverse offices — grounding humility and unity in the church.

So the witnesses make the verse the figure of the one body. The lesson the expositors draw is the diversity of the members and offices, grounding both humility and unity in the church. The witnesses note the figure: "as we have many members in one body" — the natural body has many members in one body; the figure; the body and its members; the illustration; the one body, many parts. The expositors observe the diversity of office: "all members have not the same office" — the members have not the same office (function, work); the diversity; the different functions; each member its own office; the variety in the body. The witnesses press Chrysostom's note on the purpose: Paul uses the figure (as to the Corinthians, 1 Corinthians 12) to allay haughtiness — for the body teaches that all are one, both great and small, members one of another; why then separate oneself by pride? the figure correcting pride; the body humbling the haughty; the unity rebuking the proud. The expositors draw the lesson on humility from the body: the body grounds humility — each member has its own office, none superior, none independent; the humility; no member to be proud; each in its place; the member not above the body; the humble place. The witnesses observe the unity from the body: the body grounds unity — many members, one body; the unity; the members one of another; the one body; the church one in Christ; the unity in diversity. The expositors note the application to the church: the natural body is the pattern of the church — the spiritual body of Christ, with many members, diverse gifts and offices, one body; the church the body; the pattern applied; the spiritual body; the members of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the many members, the not the same office, and the body grounding humility and unity. The verse thus records the figure of the one body with many members — teaching the diversity of the members and offices, grounding humility and unity in the church.
5:
"So we, being many, are one body in Christ, and every one members one of another." The witnesses note the church one body in Christ. Gill notes the application — the chosen, redeemed, justified, and sanctified, though few in comparison with the world and many in themselves, make up one body, the church, of which Christ is the head. The witnesses note Paul applying the figure — we, being many, are one body in Christ, and every one members one of another; the church the one body of Christ, the believers united to him as the head and to one another as fellow-members; the union with Christ and the mutual membership, the ground of the love, humility, and service that follow; the body of Christ. The witnesses note one body in Christ. The witnesses note members one of another.

The witnesses note the church one body in Christ, the believers members one of another — the union with Christ and mutual membership.

So the witnesses make the verse the church one body in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the union of the believers with Christ as the head and with one another as fellow-members. The witnesses note the application: "we, being many, are one body in Christ" — Gill notes the chosen, redeemed, and sanctified, though many in themselves, make up one body, the church, of which Christ is the head; the application of the figure; the church one body; the many made one; the body in Christ. The expositors observe the union with Christ: "one body in Christ" — the church is one body in Christ — united to him as the head; the union with Christ; the head and the body; the believers in Christ; the body of which Christ is the head; the union vital. The witnesses press the mutual membership: "every one members one of another" — the believers are members one of another — united not only to Christ but to each other; the mutual membership; the fellow-members; the believers belonging to one another; the bond between the members; the union mutual. The expositors draw the lesson on the union with Christ and one another: the believers are united to Christ (the head) and to one another (the members); the double union; the vertical and the horizontal; the union with the head and the fellow-members; the body knit together; the one body. The witnesses observe the ground of the duties: the union (with Christ and one another) is the ground of the love, humility, and service that follow (v.9-21) — for members of one body care for one another; the ground of the duties; the union the basis; the membership obliging the love; the body's mutual care. The expositors note the comfort of the membership: the believer is a member of Christ's body — united to him, belonging to the fellowship, cared for by the whole; the comfort; the belonging; the membership in Christ; the fellowship of the body; the union the comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the one body in Christ, the members one of another, and the union with Christ and the fellow-members. The verse thus records the church one body in Christ — teaching the union of the believers with Christ as the head and with one another as fellow-members.
6:
"Having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us, whether prophecy, let us prophesy according to the proportion of faith;" The witnesses note the diversity of gifts and the right use of prophecy. Gill notes that as in the natural body the members have different faculties, so in the spiritual body the church, the members have diversities of gifts, given according to grace. The witnesses note Paul teaching that, having gifts differing according to grace, each is to use his — and the prophet (preacher) to prophesy according to the proportion of faith; the diversity of gifts from grace, to be used in their place, the gift of prophecy exercised according to the proportion (measure or rule) of faith, in agreement with the truth; the right use of the gifts. The witnesses note gifts differing. The witnesses note prophesy according to faith.

The witnesses note the diversity of gifts according to grace, and prophecy used according to the proportion of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the diversity of gifts and the use of prophecy. The lesson the expositors draw is the diversity of gifts from grace, each to be used in its place, prophecy according to the proportion of faith. The witnesses note the diversity: "having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us" — Gill notes that as the body's members have different faculties, so the church's members have diversities of gifts, given according to grace; the diverse gifts; the gifts from grace; the differing endowments; the grace apportioning. The expositors observe the gifts as grace: the gifts are "according to the grace that is given" — not earned, but given of grace; the gifts of grace; the free endowments; the grace the source; the gifts not merited. The witnesses press the first gift — prophecy: "whether prophecy, let us prophesy according to the proportion of faith" — the gift of prophecy (preaching, the inspired or expounding ministry) to be exercised according to the proportion of faith; the prophecy; the preaching; the gift used; the prophesying. The expositors weigh "the proportion of faith": the witnesses note the sense — according to the measure of faith given (the degree of the gift), or according to the rule/standard of the faith (the body of truth); the prophesying in agreement with the truth, within one's measure; the proportion of faith; the rule and the measure. The witnesses observe the right use of the gift: the gift is to be used rightly — the prophet to prophesy according to the proportion of faith, in agreement with the truth, within his measure; the right use; the gift exercised properly; the prophecy according to the rule; the faithful use. The expositors note the variety of gifts to follow: the verse begins the list of gifts (prophecy, ministry, teaching, exhortation, giving, ruling, mercy, v.6-8) — each to be used in its place; the variety; the gifts enumerated; each in its office; the diverse service. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gifts differing, the prophesy according to faith, and the right use of the gifts. The verse thus records the diversity of gifts and the use of prophecy — teaching the diversity of gifts from grace, each used in its place, prophecy according to the proportion of faith.
7:
"Or ministry, let us wait on our ministering: or he that teacheth, on teaching;" The witnesses note the gifts of ministry and teaching, each to be attended to. Gill notes "ministry" here may signify the office of ministering to the poor saints (the deacon's office, distinct from prophesying), to be attended with diligence, care, and constancy. The witnesses note Paul continuing the list — let him with the gift of ministry wait on (attend diligently to) his ministering, and the teacher on his teaching; each gift to be exercised in its own sphere with diligence, the minister serving, the teacher teaching, none neglecting his gift nor intruding on another's; the faithful, diligent use of each in its place. The witnesses note wait on the ministry. The witnesses note the teacher on teaching.

The witnesses note the gifts of ministry and teaching, each attended to diligently in its own sphere — the faithful use of each gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the gifts of ministry and teaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that each gift is to be exercised diligently in its own sphere, none neglected nor intruding on another. The witnesses note ministry: "or ministry, let us wait on our ministering" — Gill notes "ministry" here may signify the office of ministering to the poor saints (the deacon's office, distinct from prophesying); the gift of service; the ministering; the waiting on the office; the diligent service. The expositors observe teaching: "or he that teacheth, on teaching" — the teacher is to attend on his teaching; the gift of teaching; the instruction; the teacher's office; the waiting on teaching. The witnesses press the waiting on the gift: each is to "wait on" (attend diligently, give himself to) his own gift — the minister to ministering, the teacher to teaching; the diligence; the attending; the giving of oneself to the gift; the faithful attention. The expositors draw the lesson on each in its sphere: each gift is to be exercised in its own sphere — the minister serving, the teacher teaching; none neglecting his gift nor intruding on another's; each in his place; the gifts not confused; the proper sphere; the order of service. The witnesses observe the diligence required: the gifts are to be waited on with diligence, care, and constancy (Gill) — not slothfully, not fitfully, but faithfully; the diligence; the constancy; the faithful exercise; the gift not neglected; the steady service. The expositors note the dignity of each gift: each gift — ministry, teaching — has its own dignity and use in the body; the minister's service and the teacher's instruction both needful; the dignity of each; the gifts honoured; each office valued; the body served by all. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the wait on the ministry, the teacher on teaching, and the diligent use of each gift in its sphere. The verse thus records the gifts of ministry and teaching — teaching that each gift is to be exercised diligently in its own sphere, none neglected nor intruding on another.
8:
"Or he that exhorteth, on exhortation: he that giveth, let him do it with simplicity; he that ruleth, with diligence; he that sheweth mercy, with cheerfulness." The witnesses note the gifts of exhortation, giving, ruling, and mercy, each with its right manner. Gill notes exhortation is a branch of preaching, more practical, giving a word of exhortation as cases require — chiefly the pastor's work. The witnesses note Paul completing the list of gifts — the exhorter on exhortation, the giver with simplicity (singleness, generosity), the ruler with diligence, the one showing mercy with cheerfulness; each gift to be used not only diligently but in its right spirit, the manner as important as the act — giving without grudging, ruling without slackness, mercy without gloom; the gifts in their proper temper. The witnesses note giving with simplicity. The witnesses note mercy with cheerfulness.

The witnesses note the gifts of exhortation, giving, ruling, and mercy — each in its right spirit; the manner as important as the act.

So the witnesses make the verse the gifts and their right manner. The lesson the expositors draw is that each gift is to be used not only diligently but in its right spirit, the manner as important as the act. The witnesses note exhortation: "he that exhorteth, on exhortation" — Gill notes exhortation is a branch of preaching, more practical, giving a word as cases require (chiefly the pastor's work); the gift of exhortation; the practical preaching; the encouraging, admonishing word. The expositors observe giving: "he that giveth, let him do it with simplicity" — the giver is to give with simplicity (singleness of heart, sincerity, liberality, without grudging or ostentation); the giving; the simplicity; the generous, sincere giving; the open hand. The witnesses press ruling with diligence: "he that ruleth, with diligence" — the one who rules (the office of leadership, oversight) is to do it with diligence (earnest care, not slackness); the ruling; the diligence; the careful oversight; the earnest leadership. The expositors draw the lesson on mercy with cheerfulness: "he that sheweth mercy, with cheerfulness" — the one who shows mercy (to the poor, the suffering) is to do it with cheerfulness (gladly, not grudgingly or with gloom); the mercy; the cheerfulness; the glad relief; "God loveth a cheerful giver" (2 Corinthians 9:7). The witnesses observe the manner as important as the act: each gift is to be used in its right spirit — giving with simplicity, ruling with diligence, mercy with cheerfulness; the manner matters; the right temper; the spirit of the service; the how as well as the what. The expositors note the proper temper of each: the proper temper crowns the gift — the giver sincere, the ruler diligent, the merciful cheerful; the temper; the spirit; the manner of the service; the gift in its right spirit; the grace adorning the gift. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the giving with simplicity, the ruling with diligence, the mercy with cheerfulness, and the manner of each gift. The verse thus records the gifts of exhortation, giving, ruling, and mercy — teaching that each gift is to be used in its right spirit, the manner as important as the act.
9:
"Let love be without dissimulation. Abhor that which is evil; cleave to that which is good." The witnesses note the call to sincere love and the abhorring of evil. Gill notes that, having addressed the officers, Paul now stirs up the exercise of grace and the duties common to all members, beginning with love — the cement of the saints, the bond of perfectness, without which all gifts are nothing. The witnesses note Paul exhorting that love be without dissimulation (unfeigned, sincere, not hypocritical), and that believers abhor evil and cleave to good; the sincere, unhypocritical love that is the root of the duties, joined to the moral bent of the renewed heart — hating evil, clinging to good; the love and the holiness of the transformed life. The witnesses note love without dissimulation. The witnesses note abhor evil, cleave to good.

The witnesses note sincere, unfeigned love, joined to abhorring evil and cleaving to good — the love and holiness of the transformed life.

So the witnesses make the verse sincere love and the moral bent of the renewed heart. The lesson the expositors draw is the unhypocritical love that is the root of the duties, joined to hating evil and clinging to good. The witnesses note the love: "let love be without dissimulation" — Gill notes Paul begins with love, the cement of the saints, the bond of perfectness, without which all gifts are nothing; love without dissimulation — unfeigned, sincere, not hypocritical; the sincere love; the unfeigned charity; the love without pretence. The expositors observe the sincerity: "without dissimulation" — the love is to be without dissimulation (hypocrisy, pretence) — real, from the heart, not feigned; the sincerity; the unhypocritical love; the genuine charity; the love unfeigned (1 Peter 1:22). The witnesses press the abhorring of evil: "abhor that which is evil" — the believer is to abhor (hate, loathe) evil; the abhorrence of evil; the hatred of sin; the loathing of the evil; the moral revulsion; the renewed heart's hatred of evil. The expositors draw the lesson on cleaving to good: "cleave to that which is good" — the believer is to cleave (cling, hold fast) to good; the cleaving to good; the holding fast the good; the clinging to the right; the attachment to good; the renewed heart's love of good. The witnesses observe the moral bent of the renewed heart: the abhorring of evil and cleaving to good is the moral bent of the transformed heart (v.2) — hating what God hates, loving what God loves; the moral bent; the renewed disposition; the holiness; the heart's bent toward good. The expositors note the union of love and holiness: love and holiness are joined — sincere love joined to the hatred of evil and love of good; the love and the holiness; the charity and the purity; the love that is holy; the union of grace and goodness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the love without dissimulation, the abhor evil, and the cleave to good. The verse thus records sincere love and the moral bent of the renewed heart — teaching the unhypocritical love that is the root of the duties, joined to hating evil and cleaving to good.
10:
"Be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love; in honour preferring one another;" The witnesses note the call to tender brotherly love and mutual honour. Gill notes this is a branch of the love just advised — the objects are "brethren," not in the natural sense, but spiritual, born of God; to whom tender, family affection is due. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to be kindly affectioned with brotherly love, and in honour to prefer one another; the tender, family affection of the saints (loving one another as brethren born of God), joined to the humility that prefers others in honour (esteeming and giving place to others above oneself); the warm love and the lowly honour of the brotherhood. The witnesses note brotherly love. The witnesses note preferring one another.

The witnesses note tender brotherly love and the humility that prefers others in honour — the warm love and lowly honour of the brotherhood.

So the witnesses make the verse brotherly love and mutual honour. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender, family affection of the saints joined to the humility that prefers others in honour. The witnesses note the affection: "be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love" — Gill notes the objects are "brethren" in the spiritual sense, born of God; the believers to be kindly affectioned (tenderly loving) with brotherly love; the tender affection; the family love; the warm charity; the brotherly love. The expositors observe the family bond: the love is "brotherly" — the love of brethren, of one family (born of God); the family affection; the brotherhood; the kindred love; the saints as brethren; the love of the family of God. The witnesses press the mutual honour: "in honour preferring one another" — the believers are to prefer one another in honour — esteeming others above oneself, giving place, showing honour; the preferring; the mutual honour; the humility that honours others; the esteeming of others; the lowly honour. The expositors draw the lesson on the humility that prefers others: to prefer others in honour is to esteem them above oneself, to give them the place of honour, to be humble; the humility; the preferring others; "in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves" (Philippians 2:3); the lowly honour; the self-forgetful esteem. The witnesses observe the warm love joined to lowly honour: the tender affection (brotherly love) is joined to the lowly honour (preferring others); the love and the humility; the warmth and the lowliness; the affection and the honour; the brotherhood marked by both. The expositors note the bond of the brotherhood: the tender love and mutual honour are the bond of the brotherhood — knitting the saints in affection and esteem; the bond; the brotherhood knit; the love and honour binding; the fellowship of the family. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the brotherly love, the preferring one another, and the warm love and lowly honour. The verse thus records brotherly love and mutual honour — teaching the tender, family affection of the saints joined to the humility that prefers others in honour.
11:
"Not slothful in business; fervent in spirit; serving the Lord;" The witnesses note the call to diligence, fervour, and service. Gill notes "business" here means not worldly affairs but spiritual business — the affairs of piety and religion, the service of God, in which we should not be slothful. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to be not slothful in business (diligent, especially in the spiritual life), fervent in spirit (warm, zealous, not lukewarm), serving the Lord; the threefold charge — diligence against sloth, fervour against coldness, and the serving of the Lord as the aim of all; the warm, diligent, God-serving life of the believer. The witnesses note not slothful. The witnesses note fervent in spirit.

The witnesses note diligence, fervour, and the serving of the Lord — the warm, diligent, God-serving life of the believer.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to diligence, fervour, and service. The lesson the expositors draw is the threefold charge — diligence against sloth, fervour against coldness, and the serving of the Lord as the aim. The witnesses note the diligence: "not slothful in business" — Gill notes "business" here is chiefly spiritual business — the affairs of piety and religion, the service of God; the believer not to be slothful (lazy, negligent) therein; the diligence; the not-slothful; the earnest application; the diligence in the spiritual life. The expositors observe the fervour: "fervent in spirit" — the believer is to be fervent (warm, zealous, boiling) in spirit — not lukewarm or cold; the fervour; the zeal; the warmth of spirit; the kindled heart; the burning, not the smouldering. The witnesses press the serving of the Lord: "serving the Lord" — all is to be the serving of the Lord — the diligence and fervour directed to his service; the serving; the Lord the aim; the service of God; the life serving Christ; the all for the Lord. The expositors draw the lesson on the threefold charge: the threefold — diligence (against sloth), fervour (against coldness), and service (the aim); the diligence, the fervour, the serving; the warm, diligent, God-serving life; the threefold mark; the active, fervent, devoted believer. The witnesses observe the warmth against coldness: the fervour of spirit is set against the coldness, lukewarmness, and deadness that creep into the religious life; the warmth; the against lukewarmness (Revelation 3:16); the fervour kept; the kindled spirit; the zeal maintained. The expositors note the serving the Lord the aim: the serving of the Lord is the aim and crown of the diligence and fervour — all directed to him; the Lord served; the aim; the devotion to Christ; the service the goal; the life for the Lord. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not slothful, the fervent in spirit, and the serving the Lord. The verse thus records the call to diligence, fervour, and service — teaching the threefold charge of diligence against sloth, fervour against coldness, and the serving of the Lord.
12:
"Rejoicing in hope; patient in tribulation; continuing instant in prayer;" The witnesses note the call to joyful hope, patience, and constant prayer. Gill notes "rejoicing in hope" (of the glory of God) is placed between serving the Lord and patience in tribulation — for nothing more animates cheerful service or patience under affliction than the hope of being for ever with the Lord. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to be rejoicing in hope, patient in tribulation, and constant (instant) in prayer; the threefold grace for the trials of life — the joy of hope that sustains, the patience that endures affliction, and the prayer that perseveres; the graces that uphold the believer through the present sufferings, hope cheering, patience bearing, prayer sustaining. The witnesses note rejoicing in hope. The witnesses note patient in tribulation.

The witnesses note joyful hope, patience in tribulation, and constant prayer — the graces that uphold the believer through trials.

So the witnesses make the verse the graces for the trials of life. The lesson the expositors draw is the threefold grace — the joy of hope, the patience that endures, and the prayer that perseveres. The witnesses note the joy of hope: "rejoicing in hope" — Gill notes "rejoicing in hope" (of the glory of God) animates cheerful service and patience; the joy of hope; the rejoicing in the prospect; the hope that gladdens; the cheering hope; the joy of the prospect of glory. The expositors observe the patience: "patient in tribulation" — the believer is to be patient in tribulation — enduring affliction with steadfast endurance; the patience; the endurance; the bearing of trials; the steadfastness under affliction; the patient suffering. The witnesses press the constant prayer: "continuing instant in prayer" — the believer is to continue instant (constant, persevering, earnest) in prayer; the constant prayer; the perseverance in prayer; the earnestness; the continuing in prayer; the persevering supplication; "pray without ceasing" (1 Thessalonians 5:17). The expositors draw the lesson on the threefold grace for trials: the three graces uphold the believer through the trials — hope cheering, patience bearing, prayer sustaining; the threefold grace; the hope, the patience, the prayer; the graces for affliction; the upholding of the soul; the trials met by the three. The witnesses observe the connection of the three: the three are connected — hope produces patience, patience drives to prayer, prayer renews hope; the connection; the graces intertwined; the hope, patience, prayer working together; the circle of grace; the mutual support. The expositors note the comfort of the graces: the believer, in trial, is upheld by hope (the prospect of glory), patience (the strength to endure), and prayer (the access to God); the comfort; the graces sustaining; the soul upheld; the trials borne by grace; the comfort in affliction. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rejoicing in hope, the patient in tribulation, and the continuing in prayer. The verse thus records the graces for the trials of life — teaching the threefold grace of joyful hope, patience in tribulation, and constant prayer.
13:
"Distributing to the necessity of saints; given to hospitality." The witnesses note the call to liberality to the needy saints and hospitality. Gill notes "distributing" (or communicating) to the necessity of saints — all in want are to be relieved, even enemies, and especially those of the household of faith. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to distribute to the necessity of the saints and to be given to hospitality; the practical charity of the body — relieving the wants of the needy saints (sharing one's substance with the poor brethren) and the open-hearted hospitality (welcoming and entertaining the saints and strangers); the love that meets material need, the fellowship of giving and welcoming. The witnesses note distributing to the saints. The witnesses note hospitality.

The witnesses note liberality to the needy saints and hospitality — the practical charity that meets material need, the love in giving and welcoming.

So the witnesses make the verse liberality and hospitality. The lesson the expositors draw is the practical charity of the body — relieving the wants of the needy saints and the open-hearted welcome of hospitality. The witnesses note the distributing: "distributing to the necessity of saints" — Gill notes distributing (communicating) to the necessity of the saints — relieving their wants; the believer to share his substance with the needy brethren; the distributing; the relieving; the sharing; the liberality to the poor saints. The expositors observe the object — the saints' necessity: the giving is to "the necessity of saints" — the real wants of the poor brethren; the necessity; the need; the saints' want; the poor of the household of faith; the needy brethren relieved. The witnesses press the hospitality: "given to hospitality" — the believer is to be given to (eager for, practising) hospitality — welcoming and entertaining the saints and strangers; the hospitality; the open home; the welcome; the entertaining of strangers (Hebrews 13:2); the open-hearted reception. The expositors draw the lesson on the practical charity: the love of the body shows itself in practical charity — relieving the needy and welcoming the stranger; the practical love; the charity that meets need; the love in deed (1 John 3:17-18); the love that gives and welcomes; the fellowship of giving. The witnesses observe the liberality of giving: the distributing is liberal, generous — sharing one's substance with the poor saints; the liberality; the generous giving; the open hand; the sharing of goods; the relief of want. The expositors note the eagerness of hospitality: "given to" hospitality means more than practising it — pursuing it, eager for it, seeking opportunities to welcome; the eagerness; the pursuit of hospitality; the seeking to welcome; the readiness; the open heart. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the distributing to the saints, the hospitality, and the practical charity of the body. The verse thus records liberality and hospitality — teaching the practical charity of the body, relieving the wants of the needy saints and the open-hearted welcome of hospitality.
14:
"Bless them which persecute you: bless, and curse not." The witnesses note the call to bless persecutors. Chrysostom notes that, having taught how they ought to be minded toward one another and joined the members closely into one, Paul next leads them forth to the battle without (toward those outside) — made easier from this point; for he who has practised well among his own will more easily deal with strangers. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to bless those who persecute them — to bless, and not curse; the hardest of the love-commands, the returning of blessing for persecution, the heart that, instead of cursing the persecutor, prays for and blesses him; the love that overcomes evil, the spirit of Christ toward enemies. The witnesses note bless them which persecute. The witnesses note bless, and curse not.

The witnesses note the call to bless persecutors — returning blessing for persecution, the spirit of Christ toward enemies.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to bless persecutors. The lesson the expositors draw is the returning of blessing for persecution, the love that overcomes evil and shows the spirit of Christ toward enemies. The witnesses note the command: "bless them which persecute you" — the believer is to bless those who persecute him; the blessing of persecutors; the love to enemies; the blessing for persecution; the hardest command. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the turn outward: having taught the duties among the brethren, Paul now leads them to the battle without (toward those outside) — easier for one who has practised love among his own; the turn outward; the love to those outside; the brethren first, then the enemies; the battle without. The witnesses press the blessing, not cursing: "bless, and curse not" — the believer is to bless and not curse — to pray for, speak well of, and seek the good of the persecutor, not to imprecate or revile; the blessing not cursing; the prayer for the persecutor; the good wished; the no cursing; the blessing returned. The expositors draw the lesson on returning blessing for persecution: the believer is to return blessing for persecution — the opposite of the natural response (cursing, revenge); the returning of good for evil; the blessing for the curse; the love to the persecutor; "pray for them which despitefully use you" (Matthew 5:44); the love that blesses. The witnesses observe the hardness of the command: this is the hardest of the love-commands — to bless those who persecute; the difficulty; the hard command; the love that costs; the flesh resisting; the grace needed. The expositors note the spirit of Christ: to bless persecutors is to show the spirit of Christ, who prayed for his murderers ("Father, forgive them," Luke 23:34); the spirit of Christ; the Master's example; the love to enemies; the Christlike heart; the imitation of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the bless them which persecute, the bless and curse not, and the spirit of Christ toward enemies. The verse thus records the call to bless persecutors — teaching the returning of blessing for persecution, the love that overcomes evil and shows the spirit of Christ.
15:
"Rejoice with them that do rejoice, and weep with them that weep." The witnesses note the call to sympathy in joy and sorrow. Gill notes rejoicing with those who rejoice (in good and lawful things, as prosperity) is a difficult task — for unless one has a near concern in another's good, he is apt to envy or repine; the call to genuine fellow-feeling. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to rejoice with those who rejoice and weep with those who weep; the sympathy of the body — the entering into one another's joys and sorrows, the fellow-feeling that shares the gladness without envy and the grief without indifference; the love that makes another's joy and sorrow its own. The witnesses note rejoice with the rejoicing. The witnesses note weep with the weeping.

The witnesses note sympathy in joy and sorrow — rejoicing with the glad and weeping with the grieving; the fellow-feeling of the body.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to sympathy. The lesson the expositors draw is the fellow-feeling of the body — sharing one another's joys without envy and sorrows without indifference. The witnesses note the rejoicing: "rejoice with them that do rejoice" — Gill notes rejoicing with those who rejoice (in good things, as prosperity) is a difficult task — for one is apt to envy or repine at another's good; the rejoicing with the glad; the sharing of joy; the gladness shared; the fellow-rejoicing. The expositors observe the weeping: "weep with them that weep" — the believer is to weep with those who weep — entering into their sorrow, sharing their grief; the weeping with the grieving; the sharing of sorrow; the sympathy in grief; the fellow-weeping. The witnesses press the difficulty of rejoicing with the glad: Gill notes rejoicing with the rejoicing is hard — for envy and repining easily intrude when another prospers; the difficulty; the envy resisted; the genuine joy in another's good; the love that rejoices without envy; the gladness shared sincerely. The expositors draw the lesson on the fellow-feeling of the body: the body shares one another's joys and sorrows — the member rejoicing with the rejoicing, weeping with the weeping; the fellow-feeling; the sympathy; "if one member be honoured, all the members rejoice with it; if one suffer, all suffer" (1 Corinthians 12:26); the body's sympathy. The witnesses observe the love that makes another's lot its own: the sympathy is the love that makes another's joy and sorrow its own — entering into the brother's experience; the love; the entering in; the sharing of the lot; the fellow-feeling of love; the brother's joy and grief made one's own. The expositors note the two harder than they seem: both are harder than they seem — rejoicing without envy, weeping without indifference; the difficulty of both; the grace needed; the sincere sympathy; the love that overcomes envy and coldness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rejoice with the rejoicing, the weep with the weeping, and the fellow-feeling of the body. The verse thus records the call to sympathy — teaching the fellow-feeling of the body, sharing one another's joys without envy and sorrows without indifference.
16:
"Be of the same mind one toward another. Mind not high things, but condescend to men of low estate. Be not wise in your own conceits." The witnesses note the call to harmony, humility, and lowliness. Gill notes "be of the same mind" is not sameness of judgment in every point, but having the same love, the same accord and affection, esteeming others better than oneself. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to be of the same mind, not to mind high things but to condescend to the lowly, and not to be wise in their own conceits; the threefold charge against pride — the harmony of mutual affection, the humility that stoops to the lowly rather than aspiring to the great, and the modesty that does not trust its own conceit; the lowliness that becomes the body. The witnesses note the same mind. The witnesses note condescend to the lowly.

The witnesses note harmony, humility, and lowliness — not minding high things but condescending to the lowly, not wise in one's own conceit.

So the witnesses make the verse harmony, humility, and lowliness. The lesson the expositors draw is the threefold charge against pride — mutual harmony, the humility that stoops to the lowly, and the modesty that distrusts its own conceit. The witnesses note the harmony: "be of the same mind one toward another" — Gill notes this is not sameness of judgment in every point, but having the same love and affection, esteeming others; the same mind; the harmony; the mutual affection; the accord; the unity of heart. The expositors observe the not minding high things: "mind not high things, but condescend to men of low estate" — the believer is not to mind (aspire to, set the heart on) high things, but to condescend to (associate with, stoop to) the lowly; the not aspiring; the condescending; the stooping to the low; the humility that goes down; the lowly association. The witnesses press the condescending to the lowly: to "condescend to men of low estate" is to associate with the humble, the poor, the obscure — not despising them nor aspiring above them; the condescending; the stooping; the love to the lowly; the humility; the going down to the low; the fellowship with the humble. The expositors draw the lesson on the not being wise in one's own conceit: "be not wise in your own conceits" — the believer is not to be wise in his own conceit — trusting his own wisdom, despising others' counsel, self-opinionated; the modesty; the not self-wise; the humility of mind; "be not wise in thine own eyes" (Proverbs 3:7); the distrust of self-conceit. The witnesses observe the threefold charge against pride: the three are against pride — the harmony (against discord), the condescending (against ambition), the modesty (against self-conceit); the threefold; the against pride; the lowliness; the humility that becomes the body; the self-abasement. The expositors note the lowliness that becomes the body: the lowliness — harmony, condescension, modesty — becomes the members of the body, who are one and equal; the lowliness; the becoming the body; the humility of the members; the lowly mind; the body's humility. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the same mind, the condescend to the lowly, and the not wise in one's own conceit. The verse thus records harmony, humility, and lowliness — teaching the threefold charge against pride, the lowliness that becomes the body.
17:
"Recompense to no man evil for evil. Provide things honest in the sight of all men." The witnesses note the call to renounce revenge and to live honestly. Gill notes "recompense to no man evil for evil" — neither evil words for evil words (railing for railing), nor evil deeds for evil deeds (one ill turn for another); private revenge is here forbidden (though the magistrate may punish evil). The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers to recompense no man evil for evil, and to provide things honest in the sight of all men; the renouncing of private revenge (not returning injury for injury) and the care to live honestly and honourably before all (giving no occasion of reproach, walking blamelessly); the no-revenge and the honest walk. The witnesses note no evil for evil. The witnesses note things honest before all.

The witnesses note renouncing private revenge and living honestly before all — no evil for evil, the blameless walk.

So the witnesses make the verse renouncing revenge and living honestly. The lesson the expositors draw is the renouncing of private revenge and the care to live honourably before all. The witnesses note the no-revenge: "recompense to no man evil for evil" — Gill notes neither evil words for evil words (railing for railing), nor evil deeds for evil deeds (one ill turn for another); private revenge forbidden; the no evil for evil; the renouncing of revenge; the not returning injury; the no retaliation. The expositors observe the qualification: Gill notes private revenge is forbidden, though the magistrate may punish evil (Romans 13:4); the private revenge renounced, not the lawful justice; the distinction; the personal vengeance forbidden, the magistrate's office allowed; the private and the public distinguished. The witnesses press the renouncing of revenge: the believer is not to recompense evil for evil — neither in word (railing) nor deed (injury); the renouncing; the no retaliation; the not avenging oneself; the evil not returned; the spirit of Christ (1 Peter 2:23). The expositors draw the lesson on the honest walk: "provide things honest in the sight of all men" — the believer is to provide (take thought for, care for) things honest (honourable, becoming) in the sight of all; the honest walk; the honourable conduct; the care for a good name; the blameless life; the walk before all. The witnesses observe the care before all men: the honesty is "in the sight of all men" — the believer to live so as to give no just occasion of reproach, to walk honourably before the world; the care before all; the good report; the blameless witness; the honourable walk; the testimony before the world. The expositors note the two joined: the renouncing of revenge and the honest walk are joined — the believer neither returns evil nor gives occasion for reproach; the two; the no-revenge and the honest life; the blameless conduct; the consistent walk. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the no evil for evil, the things honest before all, and the renouncing of revenge with the honest walk. The verse thus records renouncing revenge and living honestly — teaching the renouncing of private revenge and the care to live honourably before all.
18:
"If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men." The witnesses note the call to peaceableness with all. Barnes notes "if it be possible" implies that it cannot always be done; still it should be an object of desire; and "as much as lieth in you" implies we are to do our utmost to preserve peace and to appease the anger and malice of others. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers, if possible and as far as in them lies, to live peaceably with all men; the pursuit of peace, qualified by the two limits — "if it be possible" (for peace depends on others too) and "as much as lieth in you" (the believer's part is to do all he can); the duty of peaceableness, sought as far as conscience and others allow. The witnesses note live peaceably. The witnesses note the two qualifications.

The witnesses note the call to live peaceably with all — qualified by "if possible" and "as much as in you"; the pursuit of peace.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to peaceableness. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of peaceableness, sought as far as conscience and others allow. The witnesses note the call: "live peaceably with all men" — the believer is to live peaceably (in peace, without strife) with all men; the peaceableness; the pursuit of peace; the living in peace; the peaceable life; the peace with all. The expositors observe the first qualification: "if it be possible" — Barnes notes this implies it cannot always be done; peace depends on others too; the believer cannot always secure it; the "if possible"; the limit; the peace not always attainable; the qualification. The witnesses press the second qualification: "as much as lieth in you" — Barnes notes this implies the believer is to do his utmost to preserve peace and appease others' anger; the believer's part; the "as much as in you"; the doing of all one can; the believer's responsibility; the peace sought on one's own part. The expositors draw the lesson on the two limits: the two qualifications mark the limits — peace is to be sought, but not at the cost of truth or conscience (if it be possible), and the believer is responsible for his own part (as much as in you); the two limits; the peace qualified; the duty bounded; the peace as far as right and possible. The witnesses observe the believer's part: the believer is to do all in his power to live at peace — to be peaceable, to avoid strife, to appease anger; the believer's part; the doing of all; the peaceable spirit; the effort for peace; the responsibility owned. The expositors note that peace is not always attainable: peace cannot always be had — for it depends on others, and may not be bought at the cost of conscience; the not always attainable; the limit; the peace sometimes denied; the conscience kept; the peace not at any cost. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the live peaceably, the two qualifications, and the pursuit of peace as far as possible. The verse thus records the call to live peaceably with all — teaching the duty of peaceableness, sought as far as conscience and others allow.
19:
"Dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath: for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord." The witnesses note the call to leave vengeance to God. Gill notes the affectionate "dearly beloved" is used to move them, suggesting they were dear to him and beloved of God; and they are to avenge not themselves. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers not to avenge themselves but to give place to wrath, since God says "Vengeance is mine; I will repay"; the renouncing of self-vengeance, leaving the matter to God (giving place to his wrath, the righteous Judge), who has reserved vengeance to himself; the trust that commits the wrong to God rather than avenging it. The witnesses note avenge not yourselves. The witnesses note vengeance is mine.

The witnesses note leaving vengeance to God — avenge not yourselves, for "Vengeance is mine, I will repay"; the matter committed to the Judge.

So the witnesses make the verse leaving vengeance to God. The lesson the expositors draw is the renouncing of self-vengeance, leaving the wrong to God the righteous Judge, who has reserved vengeance to himself. The witnesses note the affectionate address: "Dearly beloved" — Gill notes the affectionate address is used to move them, suggesting they were dear to Paul and beloved of God; the tender address; the beloved; the affection; the appeal of love. The expositors observe the command: "avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath" — the believer is not to avenge himself, but to give place to wrath; the no self-vengeance; the giving place; the renouncing of revenge; the leaving room. The witnesses weigh "give place unto wrath": the witnesses note the chief sense — give place to God's wrath (leave room for God to avenge, commit the matter to his righteous judgment); some take it as giving place to the wrath of the adversary (yielding, not resisting); the chief sense — leaving vengeance to God's wrath; the place given to the Judge. The expositors draw the lesson on God's reserved vengeance: "Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord" — God has reserved vengeance to himself (Deuteronomy 32:35); the believer is not to usurp it; God the avenger; the vengeance God's; the Lord's prerogative; the righteous Judge repaying. The witnesses observe the trust in God: to renounce self-vengeance is to trust God — committing the wrong to him, the righteous Judge, who will repay; the trust; the committing; the leaving to God; the faith that does not avenge; "who committed himself to him that judgeth righteously" (1 Peter 2:23); the trust in the Judge. The expositors note the comfort and the warrant: the believer is comforted and warranted in not avenging — for God will repay; the wrong not unaddressed, but left to the righteous Judge; the comfort; the warrant; the matter in God's hands; the vengeance sure but God's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the avenge not yourselves, the vengeance is mine, and the leaving of the wrong to God. The verse thus records leaving vengeance to God — teaching the renouncing of self-vengeance, committing the wrong to God the righteous Judge.
20:
"Therefore if thine enemy hunger, feed him; if he thirst, give him drink: for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head." The witnesses note the call to do good to the enemy. Gill notes these words are taken from Proverbs 25, to be understood (as a Jewish writer observes) in their literal sense — so far should the saints be from revenging themselves, that they should do good to their enemies. The witnesses note Paul exhorting believers, instead of avenging, to feed the hungry enemy and give the thirsty drink, for in so doing they heap coals of fire on his head; the positive love that returns good for evil, the kindness that, like coals of fire, may melt the enemy to shame and repentance (or, if not, leaves him the more inexcusable); the overcoming of the enemy by kindness. The witnesses note feed thine enemy. The witnesses note coals of fire.

The witnesses note doing good to the enemy — feeding the hungry foe, heaping coals of fire; the kindness that may melt him to shame and repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse doing good to the enemy. The lesson the expositors draw is the positive love that returns good for evil, the kindness that may melt the enemy to repentance. The witnesses note the command: "if thine enemy hunger, feed him; if he thirst, give him drink" — Gill notes these words from Proverbs 25:21, to be understood literally; the believer is to feed the hungry enemy and give the thirsty drink; the doing good to the enemy; the kindness; the positive love; the good for evil. The expositors observe the going beyond non-revenge: this goes beyond not avenging (v.19) to positive good — not only refraining from revenge, but doing good to the enemy; the positive love; the good returned; the kindness to the foe; beyond non-retaliation; the active love. The witnesses press the heaping of coals of fire: "for in so doing thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head" — the witnesses weigh the sense — the kindness, like coals of fire, may melt the enemy to shame and repentance (the chief, kindest sense); or, if he remains hardened, the kindness leaves him the more inexcusable, aggravating his guilt; the coals of fire; the melting or the aggravating. The expositors draw the lesson on the kindness that melts: the chief sense is that the kindness, like coals heaping on the head, melts the enemy — shaming him, softening him, leading him to repentance; the melting; the softening; the kindness conquering; the love that wins; the enemy melted. The witnesses observe the alternative — the aggravation: if the enemy is not melted, the kindness leaves him the more inexcusable — his hardness against such love aggravating his guilt; the aggravation; the guilt increased; the kindness witnessing against him; the inexcusable hardness; the alternative effect. The expositors note the overcoming by kindness: the kindness overcomes the enemy — either by melting him to repentance or by leaving his evil powerless against the persistent good; the overcoming; the conquest by love; the good prevailing; the enemy overcome by kindness (v.21). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the feed thine enemy, the coals of fire, and the kindness that may melt the enemy. The verse thus records doing good to the enemy — teaching the positive love that returns good for evil, the kindness that may melt the enemy to repentance.
21:
"Be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good." The witnesses note the summary command to overcome evil with good. Gill notes the believer is not to be overcome by the evil one (Satan, who stirs up revenge), nor by the evil of sin within, nor by the evil of others' injuries; but to resist and conquer. The witnesses note Paul summing up the whole — be not overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good; the grand principle that crowns the section, the believer not to be conquered by the evil done to him (driven to revenge, bitterness, or retaliation) but to conquer the evil by returning good; the triumph of good over evil, the Christian victory. The witnesses note be not overcome of evil. The witnesses note overcome evil with good.

The witnesses note the summary — be not overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good; the triumph of good over evil, the Christian victory.

So the witnesses make the verse the summary command. The lesson the expositors draw is the grand principle that crowns the section — the believer not conquered by evil but conquering it with good. The witnesses note the negative: "be not overcome of evil" — Gill notes the believer is not to be overcome by the evil one (Satan, stirring revenge), nor the evil of sin within, nor the evil of others' injuries; the not overcome; the not conquered; the resisting; the not driven to evil; the not yielding. The expositors observe the positive: "but overcome evil with good" — the believer is to overcome (conquer) evil with good — returning good for evil, conquering the evil by the good; the overcoming; the conquest; the good prevailing; the evil vanquished by good; the victory of good. The witnesses press the meaning of being overcome by evil: to be overcome by evil is to be driven by the evil done to one into evil oneself — into revenge, bitterness, retaliation, hatred; the being overcome; the conquest of evil; the evil drawing into evil; the defeat; the yielding to revenge. The expositors draw the lesson on the conquest by good: to overcome evil with good is to conquer the evil by returning good — not letting the evil provoke evil, but answering it with kindness, and so conquering it; the conquest by good; the good prevailing; the evil overcome; the victory by love; the good that conquers. The witnesses observe the triumph of good: the good triumphs over the evil — either by melting the enemy (v.20) or by keeping the believer from being drawn into evil; the triumph; the victory; the good over evil; the Christian conquest; the good prevailing. The expositors note the crowning principle: this verse crowns the section (v.14-21) — the whole teaching on love to enemies summed in the grand principle; the crown; the summary; the principle; the grand rule; the Christian victory over evil. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the be not overcome of evil, the overcome evil with good, and the triumph of good. The verse thus records the summary command — be not overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good — teaching the grand principle of the Christian victory, conquering evil with good.
